          row [e401] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e402] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Complete: Countermeasure CM0001 protects mission-sensitive information such as system goals, operational modes, and scientific capabilities—precisely the information threat actors gather through REC-0009. By implementing classification, access controls, and DLP on design specifications, command databases, and failure resolution documents, this countermeasure prevents unauthorized access to mission-critical data attackers would seek to obtain." [e403] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "REC-0009->CM0001" [e404] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e405] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: REC-0009 Technique Name: Gather Mission Information Technique Description: Threat actors may initially seek to gain an understanding of a target mission by gathering information commonly captured in a Concept of Operations (or similar) document and related artifacts. Information of interest includes, but is not limited to: - the needs, goals, and objectives of the system - system overview and key elements/instruments - modes of operations (including operational constraints) - proposed capabilities and the underlying science/technology used to provide capabilities (i.e., scientific papers, research studies, etc.) - physical and support environments ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0001 Countermeasure Name: Protect Sensitive Information Countermeasure Description: Organizations should look to identify and properly classify mission sensitive design/operations information (e.g., fault management approach) and apply access control accordingly. Any location (ground system, contractor networks, etc.) storing design information needs to ensure design info is protected from exposure, exfiltration, etc. Space system sensitive information may be classified as Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) or Company Proprietary. Space system sensitive information can typically include a wide range of candidate material: the functional and performance specifications, any ICDs (like radio frequency, ground-to-space, etc.), command and telemetry databases, scripts, simulation and rehearsal results/reports, descriptions of uplink protection including any disabling/bypass features, failure/anomaly resolution, and any other sensitive information related to architecture, software, and flight/ground /mission operations. This could all need protection at the appropriate level (e.g., unclassified, CUI, proprietary, classified, etc.) to mitigate levels of cyber intrusions that may be conducted against the project’s networks. Stand-alone systems and/or separate database encryption may be needed with controlled access and on-going Configuration Management to ensure changes in command procedures and critical database areas are tracked, controlled, and fully tested to avoid loss of science or the entire mission. Sensitive documentation should only be accessed by personnel with defined roles and a need to know. Well established access controls (roles, encryption at rest and transit, etc.) and data loss prevention (DLP) technology are key countermeasures. The DLP should be configured for the specific data types in question. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." [e406] [cursor=pointer]

By implementing classification, access controls, and DLP on design specifications, command databases, and failure resolution documents, this countermeasure prevents unauthorized access to mission-critical data attackers would seek to obtain." [e407] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Countermeasure CM0001 protects mission-sensitive information such as system goals, operational modes..." [e408] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "—" [e409] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "19873ms" [e410] [cursor=pointer]

          row [e412] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e413] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Complete: Countermeasure CM0008 protects security testing reports that detail software vulnerabilities, which directly addresses how threat actors executing technique REC-0008.03 seek such vulnerability information from sources like vulnerability databases and leaked reports to exploit spacecraft systems." [e414] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "REC-0008.03->CM0008" [e415] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e416] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: REC-0008.03 Technique Name: Known Vulnerabilities Technique Description: Threat actors may gather information about vulnerabilities that can be used for future campaigns or to perpetuate other techniques. A vulnerability is a weakness in the victim spacecraft's hardware, subsystems, bus, or software that can, potentially, be exploited by a threat actor to cause unintended or unanticipated behavior to occur. During reconnaissance as threat actors identify the types/versions of software (i.e., COTS, open-source) being used, they will look for well-known vulnerabilities that could affect the spacecraft. Threat actors may find vulnerability information by searching leaked documents, vulnerability databases/scanners, compromising ground systems, and searching through online databases. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0008 Countermeasure Name: Security Testing Results Countermeasure Description: As penetration testing and vulnerability scanning is a best practice, protecting the results from these tests and scans is equally important. These reports and results typically outline detailed vulnerabilities and how to exploit them. As with countermeasure CM0001, protecting sensitive information from disclosure to threat actors is imperative. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." [e417] [cursor=pointer]
            cell "Countermeasure CM0008 protects security testing reports that detail software vulnerabilities, which directly addresses how threat actors executing technique REC-0008.03 seek such vulnerability information from sources like vulnerability databases and leaked reports to exploit spacecraft systems." [e418] [cursor=pointer]

              generic "Complete: Countermeasure CM0001 is relevant to technique REC-0008.04 because REC-0008.04 describes adversaries gathering sensitive mission information through business relationships like contractor networks, while CM0001 specifically directs protection of sensitive documentation and data 'at any location (ground system, contractor networks, etc.) storing design information' using access controls and encryption. This direct overlap in operational contexts shows why protecting sensitive information applies to business relationship vulnerabilities." [e425] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "REC-0008.04->CM0001" [e426] [cursor=pointer]
            cell [e427] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: REC-0008.04 Technique Name: Business Relationships Technique Description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's business relationships that can be used during targeting. Information about an mission’s business relationships may include a variety of details, including second or third-party organizations/domains (ex: managed service providers, contractors/sub-contractors, etc.) that have connected (and potentially elevated) network access or sensitive information. This information may also reveal supply chains and shipment paths for the victim’s hardware and software resources. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0001 Countermeasure Name: Protect Sensitive Information Countermeasure Description: Organizations should look to identify and properly classify mission sensitive design/operations information (e.g., fault management approach) and apply access control accordingly. Any location (ground system, contractor networks, etc.) storing design information needs to ensure design info is protected from exposure, exfiltration, etc. Space system sensitive information may be classified as Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) or Company Proprietary. Space system sensitive information can typically include a wide range of candidate material: the functional and performance specifications, any ICDs (like radio frequency, ground-to-space, etc.), command and telemetry databases, scripts, simulation and rehearsal results/reports, descriptions of uplink protection including any disabling/bypass features, failure/anomaly resolution, and any other sensitive information related to architecture, software, and flight/ground /mission operations. This could all need protection at the appropriate level (e.g., unclassified, CUI, proprietary, classified, etc.) to mitigate levels of cyber intrusions that may be conducted against the project’s networks. Stand-alone systems and/or separate database encryption may be needed with controlled access and on-going Configuration Management to ensure changes in command procedures and critical database areas are tracked, controlled, and fully tested to avoid loss of science or the entire mission. Sensitive documentation should only be accessed by personnel with defined roles and a need to know. Well established access controls (roles, encryption at rest and transit, etc.) and data loss prevention (DLP) technology are key countermeasures. The DLP should be configured for the specific data types in question. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." [e428] [cursor=pointer]

This direct overlap in operational contexts shows why protecting sensitive information applies to business relationship vulnerabilities." [e429] [cursor=pointer]

Threat actors identify software versions to discover known vulnerabilities (REC-0008.03), and CM0007 prevents this by hiding version numbers that could be cross-referenced with public CVE databases." [e436] [cursor=pointer]

            cell [e438] [cursor=pointer]
              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: REC-0008.03 Technique Name: Known Vulnerabilities Technique Description: Threat actors may gather information about vulnerabilities that can be used for future campaigns or to perpetuate other techniques. A vulnerability is a weakness in the victim spacecraft's hardware, subsystems, bus, or software that can, potentially, be exploited by a threat actor to cause unintended or unanticipated behavior to occur. During reconnaissance as threat actors identify the types/versions of software (i.e., COTS, open-source) being used, they will look for well-known vulnerabilities that could affect the spacecraft. Threat actors may find vulnerability information by searching leaked documents, vulnerability databases/scanners, compromising ground systems, and searching through online databases. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0007 Countermeasure Name: Software Version Numbers Countermeasure Description: When using COTS or Open-Source, protect the version numbers being used as these numbers can be cross referenced against public repos to identify Common Vulnerability Exposures (CVEs) and exploits available. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs."               generic "Complete: Countermeasure CM0005 (Ground-based Countermeasures) is relevant to technique REC-0008.04 (Business Relationships) because REC-0008.04 highlights contractor/sub-contractor networks with connected access as intelligence targets for adversaries, and CM0005 specifically identifies contractor networks as terrestrial assets requiring protection through frameworks like NIST CSF." [e447] [cursor=pointer]

              generic "Return exactly one top-level JSON object with keys: - pairs - skipped_reason Generate up to 3 grounded, non-duplicative QRA pairs about the relationship between this SPARTA technique and this SPARTA countermeasure. ## Technique Technique ID: REC-0008.04 Technique Name: Business Relationships Technique Description: Adversaries may gather information about the victim's business relationships that can be used during targeting. Information about an mission’s business relationships may include a variety of details, including second or third-party organizations/domains (ex: managed service providers, contractors/sub-contractors, etc.) that have connected (and potentially elevated) network access or sensitive information. This information may also reveal supply chains and shipment paths for the victim’s hardware and software resources. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0005 Countermeasure Name: Ground-based Countermeasures Countermeasure Description: This countermeasure is focused on the protection of terrestrial assets like ground networks and development environments/contractor networks, etc. Traditional detection technologies and capabilities would be applicable here. Utilizing resources from NIST CSF to properly secure these environments using identify, protect, detect, recover, and respond is likely warranted. Additionally, NISTIR 8401 may provide resources as well since it was developed to focus on ground-based security for space systems (https://csrc.nist.gov/pubs/ir/8401/final). Furthermore, the MITRE ATT&CK framework provides IT focused TTPs and their mitigations https://attack.mitre.org/mitigations/enterprise/. Several recommended NIST 800-53 Rev5 controls are provided for reference when designing ground systems/networks. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs." By securing design specifications, command databases, and software-related documentation with access controls and data loss prevention, the countermeasure reduces the availability of critical information that could enable software supply chain attacks like those described in the technique." This attack can take place in a number of ways, including manipulation of source code, manipulation of the update and/or distribution mechanism, or replacing compiled versions with a malicious one. ## Countermeasure Countermeasure ID: CM0001 Countermeasure Name: Protect Sensitive Information Countermeasure Description: Organizations should look to identify and properly classify mission sensitive design/operations information (e.g., fault management approach) and apply access control accordingly. Any location (ground system, contractor networks, etc.) storing design information needs to ensure design info is protected from exposure, exfiltration, etc. Space system sensitive information may be classified as Controlled Unclassified Information (CUI) or Company Proprietary. This could all need protection at the appropriate level (e.g., unclassified, CUI, proprietary, classified, etc.) to mitigate levels of cyber intrusions that may be conducted against the project’s networks. Stand-alone systems and/or separate database encryption may be needed with controlled access and on-going Configuration Management to ensure changes in command procedures and critical database areas are tracked, controlled, and fully tested to avoid loss of science or the entire mission. Sensitive documentation should only be accessed by personnel with defined roles and a need to know. Well established access controls (roles, encryption at rest and transit, etc.) and data loss prevention (DLP) technology are key countermeasures. The DLP should be configured for the specific data types in question. ## Optional Relationship Note Relationship Note: ## Reminders - This prompt is about the relationship, not standalone node definitions. - Do not infer relevance from linkage alone. - Every non-empty pair must have at least one quote from the technique and one quote from the countermeasure. - If either side is non-substantive or placeholder-like, return zero pairs. - Preserve modality exactly. - Prefer fewer pairs over weak pairs. - If source support is insufficient, return zero pairs."
